schools.summary: The opening "and" links the book to Samuel without unbroken continuity; "clothes" are bed-clothes, so David is bedridden at about seventy; Clarke dates the compilation to Ezra.
Gill computes the age — thirty at his accession, forty years a king, and so about seventy.
Henry turns the decline into a meditation on mortality: deliverances are but reprieves; use strength in youth; David chastised for former sins.

debates.question: How old was David at this time?

sides.position: sixty-nine — no advanced age, but aged in constitution

schools.summary: Keil reads the remedy as ancient medicine, judged by the customs of an age in which polygamy gave no offence; the servants are physicians.
Clarke gives the medical rationale — the young body would impart animal heat — calling it Friar Bacon's secret for old age.
Gill glosses the "servants" as physicians, as Joseph's physicians are called his servants.
Henry faults the prescription of nuptials to a dying man, yet excuses David by the ignorance of an age that had not learned Christ's law of one wife.

debates.question: How should the Abishag arrangement be judged?

sides.position: a lapse, excusable only by the ignorance of the age
nothing offensive by the custom of the time

themes: polygamy and the customs of the age

schools.summary: Ellicott locates Shunem in Issachar's plain of Jezreel, identifies it with modern Solam, and conjectures Abishag may be the "fair Shulamite" of the Song.
Gill presses the word "fair": her beauty was part of the intended remedy, that the king might endure and receive her.

outliers.note: Conjectures, "ingeniously and not improbably," that Abishag is the Shulamite of the Song of Solomon.

schools.summary: Keil: "he knew her not" is not about David's impotence but prepares for Adonijah's later request for her as wife.
Gill dwells on her fairness and her office of ministry and comfort to the bedridden king.
Clarke keeps the medical thread, making the king's continence the very condition of the cure.
Adonijah is drawn as a second Absalom — beauty, fifty runners, chariots — yet the lesser rebel; Keil frames it against God's reserved right to choose the king.
Gill: the eldest surviving and comely son, seeing his father failing, is fired with ambition to set up for king.
Henry: to insult a parent's age is base, and to force off the crown God settled on Solomon is the world's revolt against Christ's kingdom.
Gill: David humoured him and never corrected him — the very sin of Eli — and on that leniency Adonijah presumed.
Keil: such weak oversight encouraged the attempt; his beauty like Absalom and his birth after him fed his sense of claim.
Henry: those who honour sons more than God, keeping them not under discipline, forfeit the honour they expect; keep them under.
Keil traces private grievance (Joab's grudge, Abiathar's jealousy of Zadok); Ellicott reaches further — the act was not viewed as rebellion but as the eldest son's natural succession.
Clarke: Joab reckoned that making the king would keep him in command of the army, and so govern him.
Gill: the general and the high priest were the two most useful men to court, and both were discontented.

debates.question: Why did Joab and Abiathar back Adonijah?

sides.position: personal grievance and ambition — Joab's grudge, Abiathar's jealousy, self-interest
because the attempt was not seen as rebellion but as the rightful succession of the eldest

schools.summary: Ellicott identifies the loyalists — Zadok of Eleazar's line, Benaiah of the bodyguard, Nathan here as counsellor and chronicler, and the Gibborim as a picked corps.
Gill notes Zadok "bid fair" to be high priest under Solomon, as indeed he became.

outliers.note: Sets the priestly division in the frame of the older reversal — the high priesthood taken from Ithamar's line (Eli's house) and restored to the elder branch of Eleazar (Zadok).

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott map the coronation feast at the stone of Zoheleth by En-rogel, modelled on Absalom, the spring apt for sacrifice.
Clarke: with Abiathar present the animals were offered sacrificially, giving a popular, God-sanctioned colour to the pretension.
Henry: a show of devotion cloaking usurpation — "in the name of the Lord begins all evil."
Gill reads the omissions as telling: Nathan who knew the prophecy, the guard, and Solomon the rival are all pointedly left out.
Henry: Solomon uninvited whether from contempt or despair — the exclusion becomes the very proof of treason.
Keil: the same clause that closes the feast opens Nathan's counter-move; the plot is frustrated by the prophet's vigilance.
Ellicott: Nathan acts because he was the medium of the dynastic promise and of the name Jedidiah; the plotters therefore shunned him.

Henry: David, Bathsheba, and Solomon all "asleep" to the plot — Solomon resting in God who gives his beloved sleep — while Nathan, knowing God's mind, bestirs himself; Christ's ministers counsel us to save life and soul.
Gill: Bathsheba had both interest with the king and a mother's concern in the succession.
Clarke: Adonijah was now reckoned legally appointed to the regal office.
A usurper must remove his rival; Ellicott: Adonijah's crown would be sealed with Solomon's blood, and Bathsheba's favour would draw jealousy.
Clarke laments the "cursed lust of rule" that spills a brother's blood, and digresses to defend hereditary over elective monarchy.
Gill: a wise and faithful friend steadies a confounded mother with the best counsel he can give.

outliers.note: Turns from the danger into a political argument that hereditary monarchy is safer than elective.

schools.summary: The oath is unrecorded elsewhere — Ellicott dates it perhaps to Solomon's birth — and the right to name a successor lay in the king's prerogative, not birth-order.
Clarke: Bathsheba had sovereign influence over David, yet was also the living reminder of the guilt by which he acquired her.
Gill: she is to go in at once, without ceremony, and put the question plainly.

debates.question: When and where was David's oath to Bathsheba sworn?

sides.position: unrecorded, perhaps made at the time of Solomon's birth
a private oath known only to Bathsheba and Nathan

schools.summary: Ellicott: the guarded access marks the growth of Oriental royal state since Absalom, like the unapproachable throne of Persia; Keil glosses "make full" as confirm.
Henry: two agreeing witnesses would establish the word and compel a positive answer.
Gill: Nathan times his entrance to come in as she is still speaking.
Keil: the aged king kept to the inner chamber attended by Abishag; the doubled "and now" reflects the energy of Bathsheba's speech.
Henry: Bathsheba took no offence at Abishag and bowed low — a daughter of Sarah; reverence wins favour with superiors.
Gill: she took Nathan's advice and went directly to the king's apartment.
Gill: her double homage — to husband and sovereign — itself intimated she had a suit, more than an inquiry after his health.
Keil takes the obeisance as the formal opening of the whole ordered address that follows.
Henry: her address was "very discreet," beginning with a bow and waiting to be asked.
Gill: she binds the conscientious king by a solemn and binding oath sworn in the name of the LORD his God.
Henry: she knew how fast the oath would hold so conscientious a man; conscience and honour would rouse the failing king.

themes: binding the king by his word

schools.summary: Gill: she reports that Adonijah reigns but is careful to clear David, lest he think himself charged with breaking his oath.
Henry: her art is to leave David his honour while robbing him of his excuse — "thou knewest it not," but now must act.
Clarke: Adonijah was reckoned legally appointed and about to exercise his functions.
Gill: the feasting while the father lies dying is itself an insult to the king.
Henry: the pointed omission of "Solomon thy servant" shows not oversight but contempt of the act of settlement.
Clarke: the monarchy was neither hereditary nor elective — the king simply named his successor.
Gill: the throne's future will appear from the expectations of the waiting people.
Henry: Israel looks to David not only as king but as prophet — a divine sentence is in his lips — and Solomon's kingdom is a type of the Messiah's.
Ellicott: "sleep with his fathers" occurs here first in Kings and hints at death as rest, not extinction, pointing to the tombs of the kings.
Gill: to sleep with the fathers is to lie in the ancestral tomb till the morning of the resurrection.
Clarke: "counted offenders" means Adonijah will slay both mother and son once the oath is known.
Gill: Nathan entered just as Bathsheba concluded, so the two testimonies overlap and the second confirms the first.
Henry: he came seasonably to forestall a dilatory answer; David might read the timely entrance as a special providence.
Keil records Nathan's obeisance, face to the ground, and elsewhere notes he appears in this chapter as counsellor more than prophet.
Henry: he reverenced the sick king as he would the enthroned king, yet dealt more plainly than Bathsheba, to rouse the king's languor.
Gill: attendants announce the prophet at the door.

themes: a dying king is a king still

schools.summary: Ellicott: the feigned question is framed to draw out denial, assuming in court style that nothing could be done without the king's will; note the contrast of deference here with prophetic boldness elsewhere.
Gill: Nathan speaks as if he knew nothing of Bathsheba's visit, telling the story afresh.
Ellicott: "God save king Adonijah" is literally "May the king live," the acclamation used for Saul and Absalom, now stolen for a usurper.
Gill: Adonijah "goes down" from high Jerusalem to the valley of En-rogel to seize what only David can give.
Gill: "me, even me thy servant" is Nathan himself, with Zadok, Benaiah, and Solomon, all pointedly uninvited.
Henry: Nathan, intimate with the mind of God and the king, reads the exclusion as proof that neither God nor David was consulted.
Ellicott: the second feigned question, framed to draw denial, forces David to own or disown the act.
Henry: either David ordered it, faithless to God and to Nathan, or he knew nothing and the party are guilty of daring insolence — either way he must act; the timely reminder is a real kindness.
Gill: Nathan asks whether it was done with the king's knowledge, consent, and orders.
Gill: seeing Nathan confirm Bathsheba, the king acts on the settled fact of the usurpation and calls Bathsheba back.
Clarke: each witness had a separate audience, so neither could be thought to prompt the other; to Nathan the king expressed no will.
Ellicott: "the Lord that hath redeemed my soul out of all distress" is a characteristic Davidic oath, peculiarly fitting in the old man near his rest.
Gill: to the former oath he adds another for greater confirmation.
Henry: the oath is a dying saint's grateful testimony to the God who carried him through every distress, like Jacob's deathbed blessing.
Gill: David owns and confirms the truth of what Nathan and Bathsheba had alleged.
Henry: though old and failing, David remembers the sacred oath, and resolves to perform it "this day, without dispute, without delay."
Keil: "May the king live for ever" was reserved among the Israelites for occasions of special weight, though common in Babylon and Persia.
Henry: Bathsheba reverences the king while rebels affront him, and prays for the long life of the very man whose death would advance her — unselfish love.
Ellicott: a flash of David's old energy, now that the chastening for his sin is past; Keil: the royal mule signals the successor.
Gill: not Abiathar but Zadok is summoned — the high priesthood was designed for him.
Henry: the unction typifies Christ the Anointed, on whom the Spirit was poured without measure, and whose people share the anointing.
Riding the king's own mule, which no subject might use, was full public evidence of the appointed successor; Ellicott debates the site of Gihon.
Gill: the escort are David's bodyguard; the rabbis infer from "servants of your lord" that a king outranks a high priest.

sides.position: on the south, near Siloam (agreeing with the nearness of En-rogel)
on the west, near the present Jaffa gate

schools.summary: Ellicott: anointing is a religious consecration — "the Lord's anointed" — signifying the outpouring of the Spirit, the prophet's prerogative; mule, anointing, and acclamation form a threefold sanction.
Gill: either priest or prophet might anoint, as the unctions of Saul and David show.
Henry: God's king is made by unction, not feasting — "whom God calls, he will qualify"; usurpers had it not.
Keil: anointing was needed because the succession was disputed (the rabbis say none was used when undisputed); "Israel and Judah" already hints at the coming division and Israel's looser allegiance.
Clarke sets out the ceremonial steps — mule, public watercourse, anointing, trumpet, enthronement.
Gill: Solomon rides at the head, the rest following in token of subjection.

themes: the seed of the coming division

schools.summary: Keil: Benaiah's "Amen, thus saith Jehovah" prays that the king's word become God's word — no flattery but a wish God fulfilled.
Henry: the Amen is the language of faith — if we say as God says, we may hope he will say as we say by his providence.
Gill: Benaiah answers in the name of all the loyal officers.
Keil: a genuine prayer for the kingdom's prosperity, which God answered; Gill fills in its content of guidance and protection.
Clarke: to wish a son greater than his father usually wounds, yet a father welcomes it — he cites Claudian on the heir who surpasses his sire.
Henry: David did not envy his child's greatness; the godly desire those after them to surpass them.

themes: the throne of David's greater Son

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott: the orders are carried out at once, the foreign bodyguard escorting Solomon; the oil is fetched from the nearer tent on Zion.
Gill names the loyal three whom David sent.
Ellicott: the holy anointing oil of Exodus 30, taken from the nearer tent on Zion for haste; Clarke notes the horn held the oil for anointing priests and kings.
Gill canvasses at length whether the oil came from Gibeon or the tent on Zion, weighing Jarchi and Kimchi.
Henry: the horn denotes power and plenty; the anointing signifies that whom God calls he qualifies — a type of Christ the Anointed and of Christians' unction.

debates.question: From which sanctuary was the horn of oil taken?

sides.position: the nearer tent David pitched for the ark on Zion
a question left in learned suspense

schools.summary: The exuberant rejoicing ("the earth rent" — hyperbole for quaking) shows genuine, not conventional, loyalty; Ellicott: Adonijah had the great men but no popular following.
Clarke: "piped with pipes" is dancing, singing, and playing; "the earth rent" like our "rent the air with cries."
Henry: the joy declares the people's free concurrence — every Israelite indeed rejoices in the exaltation of the Son of David.

themes: the exaltation of the Son of David

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott: the shout from Gihon carries to En-rogel; the old warrior Joab first catches the trumpet while Adonijah affects unconcern.
Henry: false security amid long feasting — the kingdom was settled while they gorged, like the secure and sensual before destruction came.
Gill: these were the guests Adonijah had invited, now about to be scattered.
Keil: Adonijah suppresses his fear, greeting the runner as a worthy bringer of good news; Ellicott gives Jonathan's pedigree as a swift messenger under David.
Gill: Jonathan, son of Abiathar, had been posted in the city as a watcher for the conspirators.
Henry: usurpers flatter themselves with hopes of success; the least timorous are those in most danger.
Keil: the opening particle is "yea but" (imo vero), affirming the opposite of what Adonijah hoped.
Gill: "nay, but it is not so as you imagine — bad tidings to thee I bring."
Henry: how can those who do evil deeds expect good tidings? The worthiest man brings them the worst news.

themes: bad tidings to a bad cause

schools.summary: Keil: the threefold "and also" builds the report higher and higher toward absolute certainty, demolishing hope by accumulation of fact.
Gill follows the plain narrative of the sending to Gihon.
Keil: Jonathan names the very noise that alarmed the feast — the city ringing with Solomon's anointing.
Gill: the anointing is ascribed to both — Zadok applying the oil, Nathan declaring as a prophet that it was God's will.
Ellicott: the public enthronement in the palace follows the anointing and acclamation as an integral part of the inauguration of royalty.
Gill: the sitting on the throne is a further token that Solomon is really and actually king.
Ellicott: "the king bowed himself" is an act of worship, joining his servants' prayer and thanking God for the promise fulfilled.
Gill: the servants' blessing showed their allegiance to Solomon, leaving Adonijah nothing to hope from them.
Clarke: David, bedridden, could not see the ceremonies but had them reported by his confidential servants.
Henry: a great satisfaction to good men leaving the world to see their children rise up to serve God.
Henry: David blesses Israel's God for bringing the matter to a happy issue, his own eyes seeing his successor enthroned — the joy of a dying saint.
Gill: bowed in worship, the king was in a proper posture for this address to God.
Gill: though soldiers, the guests were struck with a panic — hearts weak as water — which "no doubt was of God."
Ellicott: the sudden collapse shows a consolidated throne, and an attempt crushed at once because it was never quite viewed as rebellion; Adonijah alone feared punishment.
Henry: combinations formed on evil principles dissolve when self-interest calls another way.
Ellicott: seizing the horns claimed sanctuary — a right the Law denied to wilful murder (later refused to Joab); Adonijah pleads his act was mere haste.
Clarke: knowing his case hopeless, he took sanctuary at the privileged altar, whose refuge it was sacrilege to violate.
Henry: he who despised Solomon as unworthy of his feast now dreads him as his judge — a warning to those who catch the altar's horns too late.
Keil: the very titles betray the reversal — Adonijah owns "king Solomon" and calls himself "his servant," and demands an oath for his life.
Henry: he that in the morning grasped at a crown is before night begging for his life.
Gill: courtiers bring Solomon word of the flight.
Keil and Clarke: life granted on condition of good behaviour — not a hair shall fall if he prove worthy, but death if wickedness be found.
Gill: to show himself worthy is to avoid all future sedition, rebellion, and treason.
Henry: clemency to a brother's first offence, yet conditional — a figure of the Son of David receiving rebels to mercy on their return to allegiance.

themes: type of the Son of David

schools.summary: Keil: Solomon would not open his reign with punishment, sparing the usurper on condition he keep quiet; "go to thy house" is not banishment from court.
Clarke: "go to thine house" barred him from place about the king, so he stood continually on good behaviour.
Henry: Solomon establishes his throne by mercy, giving the rebel life and estate — the Son of David receiving the rebellious to grace.

outliers.note: Closes his comment with a long extract from Friar Bacon's cure of old age, returning to the medical curiosity with which he opened the chapter.
